0f839359-894a-4ad8-a289-a2aea7e76407.content: This document is about wildlife and wildlife management. It defines wildlife, discusses various aspects of wildlife management, including its history and approaches, and touches on related topics such as population dynamics, habitat, and wildlife law. ( from Title: What is Wildlife?, pg. 2)
what are the related topics that was touched

The related topics touched upon are:
* Definitions of wildlife (from multiple sources like JWM 1937 and Sinclair et al. 2006) ( from Title: What is Wildlife?, pg. 2)
* The concept of management in relation to wildlife, including its active/passive, direct/indirect nature, and its connection to human domination and conservation. ( from Title: • Management indicates that human manipulations and decisions are present, pg. 4)
* Defining wildlife management, with definitions from Leopold 1933, Sinclair et al. 2006, and Bolen & Robinson 2003. ( from Title: Defining Wildlife Management, pg. 5)
* Examples of specific animals like mountain lions and wolves in historical contexts. ( from Title: Lion, pg. 8)
* Population crashes and near extinction events, specifically mentioning American bison skulls and the role of hunting in the 19th century. ( from Title: Population Crashes, pg. 8), ( from Title: Near Extinction, pg. 9)
* The extinction of species, using the passenger pigeon as an example, and its timeline. ( from Title: Extinction, pg. 10)
* Examples of extirpated wildlife such as lions in Europe, Carolina parakeets, and Hawaiian avifauna. ( from Title: Extirpated Wildlife, pg. 11)
* The primary causes of extinction: habitat loss/modification, unsustainable harvesting, and the introduction of exotic species. ( from Title: Causes of Extinction, pg. 12)
* The rise of wildlife management and policies, referencing Aldo Leopold's quote on human management. ( from Title: Rise of Wildlife Management and Policies, pg. 13)
* The history of wildlife law, from the Roman Empire to the concept of "public trust" in the United States. ( from Title: Wildlife Law, pg. 14)
* Different scales and approaches to understanding wildlife, including individuals, populations, communities, and ecosystems. ( from Title: Scales & Approaches to Understanding, pg. 15)
* Population dynamics, defining it as changes over time due to births, deaths, immigration, and emigration, and the use of demographic techniques. ( from Title: Population Dynamics, pg. 16)
* The concept of habitat, its various uses, and its core components for animals (cover, food, water). ( from Title: Habitat, pg. 20)
* A more specific definition of habitat as an area providing resources and environmental conditions that promote survival and reproduction for a given species. ( from Title: Habitat Continued, pg. 21)
